text: Hello, my name is Dr. Paul. You can also see my other two partners, Dr. Blumenthal and Dr. Christian.
We are the surgeons at the Weight Loss Surgery Center at Roger Williams Medical Center in Providence, Rhode Island.
You can reach us for appointments or find out more under the phone number, or under our website, loseweightri.com.
Today I will show you the video of a SADI-S surgery.
The medical full word is laparoscopic single anastomosis duodenal ileostomy with sleeve gastrectomy.
This is a young woman with a weight of four hundred pounds and a body mass index of seventy three.
This is the beginning of the surgery.
The surgeon begins the procedure by making six incisions on the patient's abdomen, preparing for laparoscopic access.
The surgeon marks four incisions approximately a quarter of an inch each and one or two incisions about half an inch. The belly button is visible in the middle at the bottom of the surgical field.
The surgeon introduces cannulas into the abdominal cavity, which will serve as access points for surgical instruments during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon prepares to use a total of six cannulas, which are visible as they are introduced into the abdominal area for instrument access.

The surgeon explores the abdomen after placing the cannulas, revealing the liver in the right upper corner and a significant amount of yellow fat. The fat is manipulated to ensure access to the surgical area.
The surgeon identifies the colon in the right bottom corner of the surgical field and begins measuring the intestines.
The surgeon measures the intestines starting from the colon, using an instrument marked with a black line to track the measurement, repeating the process thirty times.
The surgeon measures the intestines using a marked instrument, ensuring the correct distance of ten centimeters, approximately four inches, is maintained.
The surgeon measures the small intestines in four-inch increments, demonstrating the mobility of the intestines as they are moved backwards.

The surgeon identifies the yellow adipose tissue at the bottom of the surgical field, which contains arteries, veins, and lymphatics, demonstrating how the small intestines are attached within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon measures the small intestines, moving backwards from the colon, and marks the spot ten feet from the end with a stitch.
The surgeon places a stitch to mark the location on the small intestine, ensuring it can be easily identified later. Freehand suturing is performed to secure the stitch in place.
The surgeon prepares to measure the stomach, following the completion of previous steps involving marking and measuring the small intestines.
The surgeon identifies the end of the stomach, palpating the area to locate the anatomical boundary, which is difficult to visually discern.
And then I'll look back about an inch and a half backwards and remember that location.
The surgeon lifts the stomach on the left side to detach it, revealing the attachments on the right side that connect the stomach to surrounding structures.

The surgeon disconnects the stomach from surrounding attachments, including the pancreas, progressing upwards towards the spleen. The pink structure on the left is the stomach, which is being detached from the pancreas and other attachments until reaching the diaphragm.

The surgeon points out the liver at the top of the surgical field, noting its enlarged and heavy appearance, which hangs down over the pancreas located at the bottom. The stomach is visibly squeezed between these structures.

The surgeon navigates a challenging area on the right side where the stomach is tightly attached to the spleen. The liver is visible hanging from the top, with the magnified view enhancing the visibility of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon works in a small area, approximately half an inch to an inch in size, reaching the top part of the stomach. The diaphragm is visible as the surgeon navigates this region.
The surgeon disconnects the stomach and surrounding fat tissue from the diaphragm, clearly identifying the diaphragm during the procedure.
The surgeon has completely freed the stomach from its attachments. An endoscope is inserted to guide the dissection and assist in determining the appropriate size for the gastric sleeve.
The surgeon examines the end of the stomach using an endoscope, then gently pulls the stomach to the side to adjust its position within the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to use a stapler to detach the right side of the stomach completely, following the path of the initial tube created by the endoscope light guide.

The surgeon uses a stapling instrument to detach the right side of the stomach, thinning it to create a sleeve. The instrument cuts through the tissue and places three rows of tiny titanium staples on each side, sealing the tissue to prevent bleeding and leakage.
The surgeon continues to detach the stomach along its right side towards the diaphragm, with the liver visibly hanging down, which can complicate the procedure.

The surgeon continues to remove the right part of the stomach using a stapler, leaving the left side intact. The remaining smaller stomach, visible on the left, will hold about two to three ounces of food or fluid.
The surgeon has removed the right side of the stomach, creating a sleeve that is slightly looser than usual, allowing for some slack in the structure.
A lot of the weight loss with the Sadie comes from not absorbing the food. So it's a combination of the small stomach here and absorbing.
The surgeon maneuvers beneath the liver to finalize the creation of the long, thin stomach on the left side, resembling a banana in shape, as the procedure nears completion.
The surgeon carefully positions the staplers on the stomach, ensuring proper alignment and sealing on both sides to prevent leakage along the staple line.
If it happens, it usually causes something called peritonitis. Sepsis would require more surgeries, drains. These things could happen. Happens rarely, but it's something we have to tell patients.
It could also be too much scar tissue or kinking forming, then patients would need maybe an endoscopy, maybe some other surgery. So that's now the end of this procedure.

The surgeon prepares to remove a portion of the stomach at the end of the duodenal switch procedure. The camera view shifts to the right side of the patient, revealing the liver and the gallbladder, which appears as a large purple structure beneath the liver.
The surgeon grasps the pink section of tissue, identified as the duodenum, which is the first part of the small intestine located just behind the stomach.
The surgeon begins detaching tissue to create a tunnel behind the anatomical structures, preparing for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon prepares to cut the small intestine, initiating the rerouting process to alter the flow of food through the digestive tract.

The surgeon navigates to the top part of the duodenum, carefully maneuvering instruments near a visible bluish structure where bile ducts enter. The gallbladder is visible on the left side of the surgical field.

And sometimes the gallbladder is inflamed or has stones in, and then I'll take the gallbladder out because it's sometimes safer. And once a person has a SAIDI procedure, and then has gallbladder problems afterwards or stones in that bile tube, which is just to the right of where we're working right now, then it can be really difficult to get them out. The person may need more surgery.
The surgeon works on the lower part of the surgical field, gradually creating a tunnel behind the duodenum. The pancreas is visible adjacent to the developing tunnel.
The surgeon introduces a tunneling device to create a passage and connect to the top part of the duodenum, facilitating the rerouting of the intestinal tract.

The surgeon uses cautery to assist in creating a tunnel, ensuring it is wide enough but not excessively so. The duodenum is visible with yellow pancreatic tissue below, and subcutaneous fat beneath that. A special stapler with a distinct tip is introduced into the surgical field.

The surgeon positions the stapler within the tunnel, ensuring it is wide enough to avoid cutting blood vessels supplying the duodenum. The stapler is closed and engaged, cutting the duodenum approximately 1.5 to 2 inches behind the stomach. A small round knife is visible on top of the staple.
The surgeon observes the separation of the duodenum, with the left side remaining intact while the right side is sutured with visible black stitches to create an opening in the duodenum.

The surgeon measures the intestines, identifying a spot ten feet from the colon. An opening is made in this section of the intestines, which is then stretched slightly. A stapler is inserted into the opening to facilitate connection.
The surgeon uses a stapler to connect the duodenum to a section of the intestines, ensuring a wide opening to prevent future scarring or stricture.

The surgeon uses a stapler to create a connection between the duodenum and the intestines, with the knife visible on the left side making the incision. The stapler is removed, revealing a wide opening approximately an inch and a half in width to allow food passage. The remaining opening in the intestines is visible and requires closure to prevent leakage.

The surgeon uses another stapler to create a staple line, visibly separating the small intestine on the left from the duodenum on the right. An extra piece of tissue is removed and discarded, completing the connection between these structures.
The surgeon places additional stitches at both the bottom and top ends of the intestinal connection to relieve tension and prevent potential leakage from the staple line.

The surgeon identifies a V-shaped gap between two sections of fat, indicative of an internal hernia. To prevent intestinal obstruction, a permanent black stitch is used to close the gap, ensuring the intestines do not become kinked or blocked.
The surgeon completes the connection between the intestines and duodenum, then performs a leak test by occluding both sides of the intestines to ensure the integrity of the surgical site.

The surgeon uses an endoscope to inspect the connection at the Stuter Ridge, illuminating the area with the endoscope's light. Air and water are introduced to check for leaks, with no bubbles observed, indicating the absence of a leak.
The surgeon identifies the disconnected stomach on the right side of the surgical field, preparing to remove it from the operative area.
The surgeon carefully places the disconnected stomach into a plastic retrieval bag to prevent contact with the skin, minimizing the risk of infection. The bag is then extracted through a cannula.

The surgeon slowly pulls the large stomach through a cannula, visibly thinning it as it exits. From the outside, the stomach is extracted, leaving behind a small half-inch opening at the cannula site. The surgeon prepares to close this opening with stitches using a suturing device to prevent hernia formation.

The surgeon pulls the other side of the stitch through the tissue, using multiple stitches to secure the area. After completing the suturing, all cannulas are removed, and the surgical sites are checked for bleeding. The video shows six small incisions, with the belly button visible at the bottom of the screen.
If you're interested in learning more about the SADS surgery or other weight loss surgeries, please call us in my office, or go to our website, loseweightri.com. Thank you for watching.
